title: Chapter 10: The Inside Man
content: The laundry room at 2:30 AM was a different place than the laundry room at 10 PM.

At 10 PM, the dryers were still running — industrial thunder that masked conversation, the only privacy the institute offered. At 2:30 AM, the machines were silent. The room was still. The only sound was the hum of the fluorescent lights and the soft, almost imperceptible rhythm of five women breathing.

Vasquez stood with her back to the door, arms crossed, face unreadable. She'd traded her security uniform for civilian clothes — jeans, a dark hoodie, the uniform of someone who'd learned that authority was best worn invisibly.
"You've got ten minutes," she said. "I told the night supervisor I was doing a perimeter check. He's asleep at the desk. Won't wake up for another hour."

Chloe studied her in the dim light. Mid-forties. Latina. The kind of muscle that came from actual training, not gym membership. A scar on her left forearm — old, faded, the kind you got from something sharp and personal.
"Who are you?" Chloe asked.

"Maria Vasquez. Former Army CID. Criminal Investigation Division. Twelve years." She said it without pride, the way you'd state a fact about the weather. "I left after I realized I was investigating more victims than criminals."
"And now?"
"Now I work private security. Sterling hired my firm. I was supposed to be a routine replacement — one guard out, one guard in." Her jaw tightened. "Then I read the files."
"What files?"

"The ones they don't show patients. The intake interviews. The family referrals." Vasquez met Chloe's eyes. "I was hired to protect a medical facility. What I found was a detention center for women whose families wanted them to disappear."
Layla spoke for the first time. "You broke into Sterling's files."

"I was hired to do security assessment. That includes information security." Vasquez's mouth twitched — not a smile, but close. "They shouldn't have given me admin access if they didn't want me to read."
"What did you find?"

"Seventeen women. Not one of them meets clinical criteria for involuntary commitment." Vasquez's voice was flat, clinical, the voice of someone who'd spent twelve years writing reports that no one read. "Depression secondary to family abuse. Anxiety secondary to being threatened with institutionalization. PTSD secondary to sexual assault that families covered up. Every single diagnosis is a symptom of what was done to them — not a condition they arrived with."
Chloe felt something settle in her chest. Not relief. Confirmation.
"You have proof?"

"I have Sterling's intake database. Emails between Sterling and families. Billing records showing payments from parents for 'extended treatment' that coincided with patient refusal to comply." Vasquez pulled a USB drive from her pocket. "Everything is on here."
Layla reached for it. Her hand was trembling — not from fear, Chloe realized. From hope.
"Can we use this?" Layla asked.

"Yes," Vasquez said. "But you need to understand something. This isn't just Sterling. The families who pay him are powerful. Lawyers, executives, politicians. If this goes public, they will fight back."
"Let them," Chloe said. "We've been fighting their system our whole lives. At least this time, we have evidence."
The next morning brought news from the outside.
Vasquez, who still had unrestricted internet access, had been monitoring Anna Delgado's Twitter. The journalist had posted an update at 6 AM:

*Touring a private psychiatric facility in San Mateo County today. Asking questions about patient autonomy, family referral processes, and why a facility with a 94% success rate has zero former patients willing to speak publicly. Will report back.*

"She's coming," Chloe said, reading the tweet from Layla's tablet. Vasquez had brought the tablet to the laundry room that morning, its Wi-Fi chip now safely disabled, its data accessible via the USB drive Vasquez had provided.
"She's already here," Vasquez said. "I saw the appointment on Sterling's calendar. 10 AM. Facility tour. Meeting with Holloway. 'Prospective family member.'"
"She's going undercover."
"She's smart." Vasquez's smile was small and hard. "Sterling doesn't know she's a journalist. He thinks she's a concerned sister looking for a place to put her 'struggling' sibling."
"What happens when he finds out?"
"He won't. Not until it's too late." Vasquez leaned forward. "But we need to give her something. A signal. Something she can see during the tour that tells her we're real."
Chloe thought for a moment. Then she smiled.
"The plant," she said. "The fake plant in my room. Everyone has one. They're in every patient room, every hallway, the lobby, the cafeteria. They're the institute's signature aesthetic touch."
"What about them?"
"We're going to turn one. Just one. In the lobby. Where the tour starts."
At 9:47 AM, Anna Delgado walked through the front doors of Serenity Wellness Institute.

She was wearing a dove-gray blazer — a deliberate choice, matching the institute's color palette — and carrying a leather notebook. The notebook contained a cover story: *concerned sister, older sibling, worried about a younger sister who was "acting out" and "needed structure."*
Nurse Holloway met her in the lobby. Same calibrated smile. Same *trust me* / *obey me* handshake.
"Welcome to Serenity," Holloway said. "I understand you're interested in our program for your sister."

"She's been struggling," Anna said, her voice carefully pitched between concern and exhaustion — the exact tone of a family member who'd tried everything and was ready to hand over responsibility. "Our parents are at their wit's end. We heard your success rate is exceptional."
"Ninety-four percent. One of the highest in the country for our patient demographic."
"And what demographic is that?"
"Young women. Eighteen to thirty. Transitional life stages can be... challenging."
*Transitional life stages.* Anna filed the euphemism. The email had called it a compliance factory. She was starting to see why.

Holloway led her through the lobby, past the framed quote about surrender, past the cameras blinking their red heartbeats. Anna catalogued everything: camera placement, door locks, staff-to-patient ratio. The building was designed to look open and airy — all glass walls and natural light — but the openness was an illusion. Every glass wall was also a surveillance sightline. Every open space was visible from at least two camera angles.
It was a panopticon dressed in yoga-studio aesthetics.
And then Anna saw the plant.

The lobby had a row of potted plants — the same fake plants Chloe had identified on her first day. Identical ceramic pots. Identical plastic leaves. Identical placement along the wall like soldiers at attention.
Except one of them was facing the wrong way.
The leaves, which on every other plant pointed outward toward the room, were rotated 180 degrees. Facing the wall. Facing away from the surveillance cameras.
It was subtle. The kind of detail that no one would notice unless they were looking for a signal.
Anna was looking for a signal.
She stopped. "These plants are lovely. Are they real?"
Holloway's smile flickered — a micro-flinch, the same one Chloe had catalogued. "They're decorative. We find that greenery promotes a sense of calm."
"Of course." Anna touched the leaf of the rotated plant. "Such attention to detail."
She didn't say the words out loud, but she thought them:
*I see you. I'm here. Keep fighting.*
In the laundry room, Chloe waited.

Layla had managed to access a single camera feed — the lobby camera, the one that covered the front desk. It was the only feed Vasquez had been able to unlock before Sterling's security audit locked everything else down. They couldn't see the whole building, but they could see the lobby.
And on the grainy tablet screen, they watched Anna Delgado stop at the rotated plant and touch its leaf.
"She saw it," Maya breathed. "She saw the signal."
"She's in," Chloe said. "Now we just need to survive until she gets out."
Anna's tour lasted forty-five minutes.

She saw the cafeteria — empty, mid-morning, the tables identically arranged. She saw the therapy rooms — beige, windowless, each with a whiteboard listing the day's prompt. She saw the garden — beautiful, manicured, surrounded by a fence that was "for patient safety."
She did not see the second floor. Holloway said it was restricted to "advanced patients."
She did not see the medication station. Holloway said it was "a clinical area, not appropriate for tours."
She did not see any patients. The tour was scheduled during Group Therapy C, when every patient was in a room with no windows and a locked door.
Sterling had designed the tour route like he'd designed everything else: to show exactly what he wanted you to see and nothing else.
But Anna had been a journalist for fifteen years. She knew that what someone didn't show you was more important than what they did.
At the end of the tour, Holloway handed her a brochure. "We'd love to schedule an evaluation for your sister. I'm confident we can help her."
"I'm sure you can," Anna said, and smiled. "I'll be in touch very soon."
She walked out of Serenity Wellness Institute with the brochure in one hand and her phone in the other, already typing the headline that would run on The Verge's front page:
*THE DISAPPEARANCE FACTORY: INSIDE THE PRIVATE PSYCHIATRIC FACILITY WHERE WEALTHY FAMILIES MAKE THEIR DAUGHTERS DISAPPEAR*
That night, in Room 214, Chloe lay on her bed and stared at the camera.

Eleven days. She'd been inside for eleven days. In that time, she'd built an underground network of five women, stolen the institute's own technology, sent a flare to a journalist who had now walked through the front doors and seen their signal, and acquired a USB drive containing evidence that could put Marcus Sterling in prison.
And she'd done it all while the institute thought she was journaling about her feelings.
The camera blinked.
"We're going to win," she whispered.
The camera blinked again.

And somewhere on the second floor, Rachel Okonkwo — three years inside, three years memorizing files, three years waiting for someone to come — pressed her palm against the window and watched the taillights of Anna Delgado's car disappear into the redwoods.
She didn't smile. She hadn't smiled in three years.
But her hand, pressed against the glass, was no longer still.
It was trembling.
Not from fear.
From something she'd almost forgotten existed.
Hope.
